articles.desc: Global patterns in leaf size are linked to climatic trade‐offs, so we explore these relationships in a major group of plants, the conifers. Batesian mimicry is one such interaction in which mimic protection is often assumed to depend on temporal and spatial overlap with defended models, although recent studies suggest strict synchrony may not always be required. articles.desc: Abstract Since its consolidation as a sub‐discipline of ecology in the late 1990s, urban ecology has grown substantially, enhancing our understanding of how urbanization shapes biodiversity and how biodiversity adapts to urbanization. articles.title: Uncovering the unequal geography of climate change
articles.desc: Nature Climate Change, Published online: 30 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41558-026-02674-1 Understanding the socioeconomic impact of climate change at fine scale is essential for promoting real-world actions. Here I look back at a 2018 paper that disaggregated the global economic damages from climate change and discuss how high granularity analyses advance climate impact research and policy progress.

articles.title: Residual emissions may perpetuate community-scale inequalities in US air pollution
articles.desc: Nature Climate Change, Published online: 30 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41558-026-02675-0 The scale of carbon dioxide removal (CDR) could determine the extent of co-emitted air pollutants in net-zero scenarios and potential health impacts. By linking a series of models and datasets, researchers find that low-CDR pathways lead to a more equitable distribution of health benefits across the USA.

articles.title: Extreme heat and the limits of tree and forest resilience
articles.desc: Nature Climate Change, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41558-026-02679-w The 2021 Pacific Northwest heat dome shows how acute thermal stress challenges prevailing assumptions about ecological resilience and adaptation. Extreme heat events are revealing physiological limits in forests that are not captured by conventional climate risk frameworks.

articles.title: The continuous global greening under climate change
articles.desc: Nature Climate Change, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41558-026-02681-2 Global greening has persisted under climate change, with feedbacks for Earth’s future climate. Here I look back on a critical 2016 study that resolved the patterns and drivers of global greening and consider how this work influences studies to monitor, model and manage greening.

articles.title: Mental health as both outcome and determinant in climate adaptation
articles.desc: Nature Climate Change, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41558-026-02673-2 Mental health should not be viewed solely as a passive outcome of climate adaptation. Rather, it serves as a key determinant of cognitive capacity and shapes the effectiveness of climate adaptation. Here we call for the integration of mental health into adaptation assessments and policy implementation.

articles.title: Pathogen-induced TaMP relocalization to chloroplasts promotes wheat susceptibility to rust
articles.desc: Nature Plants, Published online: 01 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41477-026-02331-7 Chloroplasts have key roles in plant immunity, but how external immune signals reach these organelles is unclear. We identified TaMP, a wheat protein that relocalizes from the plasma membrane to chloroplasts in response to pathogen infection, and found that a rust effector hijacks this process to promote TaGluTR degradation and increase wheat susceptibility to rust.

articles.title: The cytosolic arginine pool represses the progression of leaf senescence
articles.desc: Nature Plants, Published online: 01 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41477-026-02330-8 How senescing cells time their transition to the cell death programme is unclear. We show that, in Arabidopsis thaliana, senescing cells are genetically wired to dissipate their cytosolic arginine pool. This pool serves as a proxy for the efficiency of nitrogen remobilization, enabling precise timing between the remobilization and termination phases of senescence.

articles.title: The progression of leaf senescence is gated by the cytosolic arginine pool
articles.desc: Nature Plants, Published online: 30 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41477-026-02328-2 This work provides compelling evidence that the progression of leaf senescence is gated by the cytosolic arginine pool and that senescing cells are genetically wired to dissipate this pool. It is proposed that the cytosolic arginine pool serves as a proxy for nitrogen remobilization efficiency, thereby enabling precise timing of the remobilization and termination phases.

articles.title: N-myristoylation-mediated shuttling of TaMP from plasma membrane to chloroplasts increases wheat susceptibility to rust fungi
articles.desc: Nature Plants, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41477-026-02324-6 A rust effector interacts with a plasma membrane-anchored N-myristoylated protein TaMP to hijack and promote its relocalization from the plasma membrane to chloroplasts, increasing wheat susceptibility to rust fungus.

articles.title: New tools crack repetitive cane
articles.desc: Nature Plants, Published online: 23 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41477-026-02337-1 A fully haplotype-resolved sugarcane genome reveals the genetic architecture of sugar accumulation, yet the transposable elements that make up most of that genome remain a largely unread layer of regulatory variation.

articles.title: Somatic embryogenesis resets the epigenetic cold memory
articles.desc: Nature Plants, Published online: 22 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41477-026-02320-w The long-standing view that the vernalized state is faithfully inherited through asexual reproduction is overturned by the finding that somatic embryogenesis resets parental cold memory.

articles.title: Protecting plants, protecting lives
articles.desc: Nature Plants, Published online: 22 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41477-026-02335-3 To address future agricultural and ecological challenges, plant protection requires integrative efforts spanning policy-making, scientific and technological innovations, as well as public engagement.

articles.title: Resetting of epigenetic cold memory through somatic embryogenesis in plant regeneration
articles.desc: Nature Plants, Published online: 22 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41477-026-02325-5 This study shows that the epigenetic cold memory or ‘vernalized state’ in overwintering plants can be reset through somatic embryogenesis, overturning the long-standing idea that the vernalized state cannot be reset via asexual reproduction.
